content.text: What is API Serving for Agents?

API serving for agents is the discipline of exposing an agent's capabilities through a well-defined HTTP (or gRPC) interface so that clients — browsers, mobile apps, CLI tools, or other agents — can invoke, monitor, and receive results from that agent over the network.

Instead of running the agent inline inside the caller's process, an agent API server decouples the agent runtime from its consumers. Clients send requests; the server starts the agent, manages its tool calls and LLM interactions, and returns the result — synchronously, as a token stream, or through a job polling mechanism for long-running tasks.
The three common response patterns are:
(server pushes token chunks as the LLM generates them), and
immediately and the client polls for completion).
Choosing the right pattern depends on expected latency — agents that finish in under five seconds can be synchronous, while agents that run for minutes require the async job pattern.
Agents frequently take far longer than a standard web request. An LLM call alone can take two to thirty seconds; an agent that also searches the web, reads files, and executes code can take minutes.

Without a proper serving layer, callers must either block indefinitely (risky for load balancers with thirty-second timeouts), poll ad-hoc endpoints that were not designed for it, or deploy the agent as a library — preventing reuse across services.
A dedicated agent API server solves this by giving the agent a stable contract: a versioned URL, structured request and response bodies, authentication, rate limiting, and observability hooks.
It also unlocks horizontal scaling — multiple replicas of the agent server can run behind a load balancer with no client changes.
any client that can make an HTTP request can invoke the agent, regardless of the language the agent is written in.
a single agent service can be called by many different products without embedding the agent code in each.
the agent tier scales independently of the client tier — add agent server replicas when load increases without touching the caller.
SSE lets the UI render tokens as they arrive, turning a thirty-second wait into a live typing experience.
API keys, JWT verification, and per-key rate limits live in one place rather than being reimplemented in every caller.
HTTP servers expose natural hook points for tracing (OpenTelemetry), logging, and metrics (Prometheus). Request IDs correlate client calls to agent traces.
) lets you evolve the agent API without breaking existing integrations.
adding an HTTP layer introduces serialization and network round-trips. For sub-second tasks this overhead is material.

SSE requires the server to hold open an HTTP connection per streaming client. Behind many load balancers or proxies the default timeout (30–60 s) kills the stream before the agent finishes. This requires configuring proxy timeouts or using WebSockets instead.
async jobs need durable storage (Redis, Postgres) to track status. If the agent server crashes while a job is running, the job is lost unless it is backed by a proper queue.
spinning up a new agent server process for the first request can take seconds if the agent loads large models or initializes connections.
an exposed API multiplies LLM spending if callers can trigger unbounded agent runs. Rate limiting and per-user spending caps are mandatory, not optional.
if a client retries a request due to a network timeout, the agent may run twice and perform duplicate side effects (sending an email, charging a card). Idempotency keys are the standard mitigation.
When a client sends a POST request to
, the API server validates the API key, parses the request body, and hands the task to the agent runtime.
The runtime runs the agent loop: it calls the LLM, receives tool calls, dispatches them, feeds results back to the LLM, and repeats until the agent produces a final response or hits a step limit.
For synchronous endpoints the server holds the HTTP connection open, waits for the agent to complete, and writes the JSON response.
For SSE endpoints the server writes
lines to the response as the LLM emits tokens — the client reads these using the browser's EventSource API or an SSE library.
For async endpoints the server enqueues the task in a job queue (Redis, Celery, Temporal), returns

attrs.alt: API Serving for Agents architecture: Clients -> API Gateway -> Agent API Server (sync, SSE, async endpoints) -> Agent Runtime -> External services (LLM, vector DB, tools)

content.text: use when the agent task reliably completes in under five seconds and the caller can tolerate blocking. Classification, short Q&A, and single-turn summarization often fit here.
use when the agent's LLM calls generate text the user should see live. Chat interfaces, document drafting, and code generation benefit most.
use for agents that perform multi-step research, run code, browse the web, or otherwise take tens of seconds to minutes. This is the correct default for autonomous agents.
use for interactive, full-duplex sessions where the client can send mid-run messages — for example, human-in-the-loop agents that ask the user a clarifying question and then continue.
use the async pattern when one agent fans out to sub-agents. A supervisor agent issues job requests for each sub-agent and collects results when they complete.
Decide what the caller must send:
(model, max_steps, tool_filter). Keep v1 minimal — add fields in later versions.
FastAPI is the standard Python choice — async support, automatic OpenAPI docs, and Pydantic validation. Flask works for simpler cases. For gRPC use Protocol Buffers and the grpc library.
Await the agent runner and return the result as JSON. Set a hard timeout (
or an ASGI middleware) so a runaway agent does not exhaust worker capacity.
Use an async generator to yield token chunks. In FastAPI return a
On POST, enqueue the job (Celery task, Redis stream, or Temporal workflow). Return 202 with a stable
. On GET, look up the job status from storage and return
in a FastAPI dependency. Return 401 for missing credentials and 403 for valid but unauthorized keys.
Use a sliding-window counter in Redis keyed by API key. Return 429 with a
header when the limit is exceeded.
Instrument with OpenTelemetry — start a trace span per request, add the
as a span attribute, and propagate trace context into the agent runtime.
Nginx or a cloud load balancer handles TLS termination. Set
(or equivalent) to support long-running streaming and sync responses.
Async job endpoints with Redis and Celery

from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel
import uuid, redis.asyncio as aioredis, json

app = FastAPI()
redis_client = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379")

class JobRequest(BaseModel):
    task: str
    context: list[dict] = []

class JobStatus(BaseModel):
    job_id: str
    status: str          # queued | running | done | failed
    result: str | None = None
    error: str | None = None

@app.post("/v1/agent/jobs", status_code=202)
async def create_job(req: JobRequest, api_key: str = Depends(verify_api_key)):
    job_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    await redis_client.set(
        f"job:{job_id}",
        json.dumps({"status": "queued", "result": None, "error": None}),
        ex=3600,   # expire after 1 hour
    )
    # Enqueue in Celery (or any worker system)
    run_agent_task.delay(job_id, req.task, req.context)
    return {"job_id": job_id, "status": "queued"}

@app.get("/v1/agent/jobs/{job_id}", response_model=JobStatus)
async def get_job(job_id: str, api_key: str = Depends(verify_api_key)):
    raw = await redis_client.get(f"job:{job_id}")
    if raw is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Job not found")
    data = json.loads(raw)
    return JobStatus(job_id=job_id, **data)

# --- Celery worker (separate process) ---
from celery import Celery
import asyncio, redis as sync_redis, json as sjson

celery_app = Celery("agent_jobs", broker="redis://localhost:6379/0")
sync_redis_client = sync_redis.Redis()

@celery_app.task
def run_agent_task(job_id: str, task: str, context: list):
    sync_redis_client.set(f"job:{job_id}", sjson.dumps({"status": "running", "result": None, "error": None}), ex=3600)
    try:
        result = asyncio.run(agent_runtime.run(task, context))
        sync_redis_client.set(f"job:{job_id}", sjson.dumps({"status": "done", "result": result.text, "error": None}), ex=3600)
    except Exception as e:
        sync_redis_client.set(f"job:{job_id}", sjson.dumps({"status": "failed", "result": None, "error": str(e)}), ex=3600)
A research product lets users ask a question and receive a multi-paragraph answer generated by an agent that searches the web, reads top results, and synthesizes a cited response.

The task takes 30–90 seconds, so the sync pattern is not viable. The product uses SSE so the user sees the answer appear token by token, with tool-call events shown as "Searching for X..." captions in the UI.
On the server, every SSE response gets a unique request ID returned in the header.
The agent runtime creates a child OpenTelemetry span for each tool call with the tool name and input size as attributes.
If the agent fails mid-stream, the server sends a final
so the client knows the stream ended abnormally.
is 60 seconds. An agent that takes 90 seconds silently closes the SSE connection. Set the timeout to at least 300 seconds and add
if a client retries a timed-out POST, the agent runs twice and may send duplicate emails or execute duplicate trades. Accept an optional
header and cache the response for 24 hours.
calling a synchronous LLM SDK method (
) inside an async FastAPI handler blocks the entire event loop. Use
Returning the full agent trace to the caller:
tool inputs often contain sensitive retrieved content (PII, private documents). Return only the final result and summary to clients by default; offer a debug trace only to privileged callers.
guard can loop indefinitely on an ambiguous task, consuming LLM tokens and blocking a worker. Always enforce an upper bound.
returning 202 Accepted without persisting the job to durable storage means the job is lost if the server restarts. Write job state to Redis or a database before returning.
changing the response schema without a version bump breaks existing callers. Use
in the URL from day one. Introduce breaking changes only in
rather than plain strings. Clients can branch on the code without parsing the message.
generate a UUID per request and return it as
in the response header. Log it in every line the agent emits. This makes tracing a specific user complaint trivial.
(readiness — checks downstream connectivity). Kubernetes uses these to route traffic correctly during deploys.
on SIGTERM, stop accepting new requests but let in-flight agent runs complete before exiting. FastAPI's lifespan context makes this straightforward.
use a sliding-window counter in Redis keyed by API key and endpoint. Separate limits for sync (high concurrency allowed) and async jobs (bounded by worker capacity).
validate task length, context message count, and max_steps before starting the agent. Reject clearly malformed requests at the boundary before spending any LLM tokens.
do not run agent logic inside the HTTP server process for async jobs. Use a separate worker process pool (Celery, ARQ) so a crashing agent does not take down the API server.
Agent API servers are almost always I/O bound rather than CPU bound — the bottleneck is LLM API latency and downstream tool calls, not local computation.
) a good fit: a single process can serve hundreds of concurrent streaming responses, each awaiting LLM tokens.
For the async job pattern, scale the worker pool independently of the API tier. Add Celery workers when the job queue depth rises above a threshold.
The API server scales to handle increased polling traffic (cheap GET requests) while workers handle the expensive agent runs.
reuse HTTP connections to the LLM API and tool backends.
keeps a connection pool alive across requests. Creating a new connection per LLM call adds 100–300 ms of TLS handshake overhead.

for deterministic tasks, cache the result by a hash of the task and context. Cache in Redis with a short TTL (5–60 minutes). This is especially useful for batch processing where many rows ask similar questions.
run multiple stateless API server replicas behind a load balancer. Sessions are not sticky — any replica can handle any request. Job state lives in Redis, not in the server process.
use KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) or a cloud equivalent to scale the Celery worker deployment based on queue depth. Scale to zero during off-hours to reduce cost.
Async-native, auto OpenAPI docs, Pydantic validation
Uvicorn for development; Gunicorn + Uvicorn workers for production
Use AsyncClient for LLM and tool calls inside the agent
Mature, widely supported; Redis as broker and result backend
Lighter weight than Celery; native asyncio, Redis-backed
Best for long-running, resumable agent workflows
Auto-instruments FastAPI; export to Jaeger, Honeycomb, or Datadog
Track request rate, P99 latency, job queue depth, LLM token usage
TLS termination, streaming timeout config, rate limiting
Centralized auth, rate limiting, and routing for multi-service architectures
Define request/response models; reject malformed inputs before they reach the agent
Single-service apps where sharing is unnecessary
Chat, drafting — users expect live output
Fire-and-forget pipelines with no polling API
An exposed agent API is a spending multiplier: every unauthenticated request can trigger LLM calls that cost real money.
Authentication and rate limiting are non-negotiable from the first deploy.
generate keys with at least 256 bits of entropy (use
). Store only the hash of the key (bcrypt or SHA-256) in the database. Support key rotation without downtime by accepting a short overlap window during which both old and new keys are valid.

reject tasks that exceed a maximum character length. Validate context message structure. Malicious inputs designed to hijack the agent (prompt injection) are harder to prevent at the API layer, but length and structure checks reduce the attack surface.
track LLM token usage per API key per day in Redis. Return 429 when a key hits its daily budget. Surface per-key usage in a billing dashboard.

the agent runtime may call external services using service-level credentials (database passwords, third-party API keys). These must not be accessible to the agent's LLM — pass them as function arguments to tool implementations, not as context.
enforce both a per-step LLM timeout (e.g., 60 s) and a total task timeout (e.g., 300 s). An agent stuck in an infinite tool-call loop should be terminated, not left running.
log task text and context hashes (not raw content if it may contain PII) at the INFO level. Log failures and step-limit violations at ERROR with the full job ID for post-mortem debugging.
if the server sends results to a caller-provided webhook URL, sign the payload with HMAC-SHA256 and include a timestamp so the receiver can verify authenticity and reject replays.
Agent API serving exposes agent capabilities over HTTP using three patterns: synchronous (task < 5 s), SSE streaming (live token output), and async job + poll (long-running tasks).
as the default for autonomous agents — most real-world agents run longer than any proxy timeout.
SSE streaming requires specific proxy configuration: increase
to prevent Nginx from buffering the stream.
Authentication, rate limiting, and idempotency keys
are mandatory before exposing an agent API — unguarded endpoints burn LLM budget and may execute side effects multiple times on client retry.
(Celery, ARQ, Temporal) for async jobs — a crashing agent must not kill the API server.
and an async LLM SDK inside the API server process to serve hundreds of concurrent streaming responses without blocking the event loop.
Version the API from day one with a
URL prefix. Breaking changes always go to
rather than silently modifying the existing contract.
Instrument with OpenTelemetry from the start — distributed traces that span the HTTP request, agent loop, and tool calls are essential for debugging slow or failing agent runs in production.
